replies.text:
Not including any of the OT cast would have made for a better film if you think about it.

Palpatine and the Last Order manage to gain enough strength to build a super weapon that makes the two death stars (which took decades to plan develop and build) obsolete without anyone ever noticing.  By having this happen during the lifetime of Han, Luke and Leia it retroactively makes their struggles in the OT pointless, as Palpatine doesn't die, the Empire doesn't fall, Han and Leia's romance means nothing and the strong leader of the rebellion is turned into an incompetent ruler as she fails to notice the presence of an emerging empire and massive super weapon being built under her own nose.  Also, she can't hold onto her relationship with Han and her son goes full dark side so she's failed as a wife and mother as well.  The empire ultimately doesn't fall because of the skywalker clan, it falls because of Palpatine's grand-daughter coming out of nowhere stealing everyone's thunder.

Had this have been set several generations after the OT it would have sucked slightly less, but forcibly undoing the OT cast's character resolutions for the sake of some sad cameos made a mediocre trilogy worse.  A young planet-hopping bounty hunter is cool, a sixty-year-old divorcee planet hopper is just depressing.

replies.text: RickTaylor215 It worked for Lost because people didn't realize there would be no answers for the mystery boxes.  People love suspense and mystery in writing, it's what gets people interested in your story.  But if there's no payoff for those questions being presented, people are going to get annoyed real quick.  The OP is right, the mystery box is just an incredibly lazy way to write stories. "Here's a puzzling situation to get you hooked. What could it possibly be?  Well, I'm not going to bother explaining it to you. That would require intelligent writing."

I feel like mystery box writing only really works with episodic tv shows. Where you don't have to have these long plot threads and can focus on the main plot while maybe stringing together a connection throughout the whole series to pay off everything.
The problem is, while this is great for the START of a show or movie. It's terrible after. You can start a show off with a big question, draw an audience in, then once you know an audience exists, write the rest and then end it off satisfyingly. But JJ overuses this too much, he KEEPS trailing the mystery box without actually paying it off.

replies.author: @babobereta
replies.text: Lost is a perfect example of why that mystery box writing style doesn`t really work, and that especially applies when it comes to movies. You get people hooked up by setting up all these questions and mysteries but instead of resolving them, you just continue to set up more and more twists and it becomes so convoluted that by the end it`s impossible to have a coherent explanation that answers everything and gives a satisfying payoff. It becomes obvious that writers are just making it up as they are going and don`t really have a clear plan of what they are doing. It`s just that, when it came to the show, people invested so much time into it that they refuse to accept it and are desperate to find some meaning so they pretend that whoever doesn`t like it just doesn`t "get it". When you do that in a movie, which is a shorter self contained story it`s much more obvious that it`s just total bs

replies.author: @babobereta
replies.text: RickTaylor215 It worked because people naturally enjoy mysteries and coming up with theories and possible explanations waiting for the next episode while the show was airing. But that is with the assumption and expectation that you will get a satisfying explanation and resolution in the end. I watched Lost (but I binged it after it aired, I didn`t watch it when it aired originally). It becomes painfully obvious somewhere in the second season that this is just total bs and they were just making it up as they were going. As I said, mystery box is a good way of hooking people, but that was different time when that concept wasn`t overused and people didn`t really know what they were getting into. If J.J. Abrams tried to do a show like that today when his gimmick is well known, I doubt it would have success it had. Also, writers lied about it in the interviews saying they had a plan and explanation for everything. And then you get to the last season and nothing gets resolved, it`s just bs vague mystery "interpret it yourself" explanations we already got. But people don`t want to admit it to themselves because they invested so much time into it, so if you say you don`t like it they hit you with "you don`t get it" or "it was never about mysteries, it was about characters". Well what was the point of all the mysteries then? You have dozens and dozens of plot points and different questions that played a major part in the story at some point that never got answered or resolved in any way. And many people today still claim it`s one of the greatest shows ever, give me a break. They didn`t answer any questions because there was no coherent way they could have, they were just riding show`s success and milking it for everything they could, stretching that mystery box as far as they could without having a clear plan of what to do in the end and coming up with the most pathetic cop out "it was all about characters" ending.

replies.author: @babobereta
replies.text: RickTaylor215 If i remember correctly show was originally supposed to run for just one or two seasons, that`s what was planned. But since it got really successful overnight they decided to expand it, stretch it out and milk it as much as possible as long as it had good ratings. If they stuck with the original plan of just couple of seasons with a satisfying conclusion and explanation, it could have been a great show. But I have to judge it for what it is in full, I can`t just look at the first season without considering everything that came after. It was their decision to water it down and push quantity over quality. So it is what it is in the end, I`m gonna remember it as a bad show that initially perhaps had some decent potential, but as I already said, a perfect example of why Abrams "mystery box" writing style doesn`t really work and is just a cheap gimmick designed to trick viewers. I`m actually glad I didn`t watch it when it originally aired because that would have been such a disappointment in the end.

author: @2down4up
text: I turned off of Star Wars in episode seven. When Rey went from a scavenger nobody to rivaling the skills of a Sith lord after one botched interrogation attempt that looked more like two constipated actors trying to see who could empty their bowels first. Or at least that’s how I remember it. The sudden change was so jarring that I literally asked my girlfriend if I had unknowingly fallen asleep and missed some crucial part of the plot. When she confirmed I hadn’t, I watched the rest of the movie hoping for an explanation. Upon finding none, I checked out and never went back.

replies.author: @robindude8187
replies.text:
For me, I got bored about half an hour into the movie. But... I love Star Wars, so... I watched. I wasn't thrilled by the end of it. So I watched the next one. Okay, that... was less than good. It was boring, and where it wasn't boring it wasn't really making sense. So I turned to the next one hoping they'd fix it. And then the opening crawl happened and I knew it was over. Just... over. There was no coming back, no redemption possible for this trilogy, no way to fix it. I watched, hoping I was wrong, but every minute that went by my hopes got lower, and lower, and lower... until we got to the end where space ships couldn't figure out where "up" was, or triangulate off each other, or realize they could rotate to the side to dump those on the hull off of themselves. Except, of course, that _other space ships_ were able to figure all this out _just fine_ when they swarmed in.

The only way forward now would be a total reboot. Pretend the sequel trilogy never happened, ignore Rey, ignore BB8, don't even bring back C-3P0 or R2D2. Take the universe as a premise, start again, and move on. Everything else is tainted beyond recovery.

replies.author: @SPQRTempus
replies.text:
I didn't know what a Mary Sue was until I saw Force Awakens.  Some how a scavenger who didn't even own a speeder suddenly became an expert pilot and gunner, and could best a Sith Lord in a lightsaber duel with relative ease.  Something didn't add up.  But to JJ's credit,  there were some missed opportunities there to really do something interesting with the character arcs and story.  To this day I still don't know why Finn is relevant to the central plot,  but I have realized in actual fact he isn't.  He does nothing of any great relevance.  When in TLJ he tried to sacrifice himself I thought,  "cool,  his heroic death would really mess with Rey's feelings that could see some dark side temptation similar to what happened to Anakin with Padme,"  but no,  it was simply a vehicle for another pointless character Rose to do something equally irrelevant to the central plot.
In my mind, the best new Star Wars film since episode VI was and is Rogue One.  Disney should have done themselves a favour and put those filmmakers in charge of the entire franchise because they understand what Star Wars is.

replies.author: @laRoz67
replies.text: @journeyburney I was a buyer for props/art dept for 6 weeks. As the reshoots for Rogue One were happening at the same time in the same studio (Pinewood) I was shared across both productions. I bought a crystal which was supposed to be a Kyber (?) crystal for Luke's broken light sabre after he'd destroyed it - I don't think that scene got shot - but it became the crystal in the necklace that Felicity Jones' character wore in Rogue One. It was an Aquamarine, and I bought it in a gem shop in London's Kings Road for £120. In my personal opinion Rogue One was the only film worthy of the Star Wars name after the Disney acquisition and I'm very proud of my very small part of that. I also dropped off a few bits and pieces to stage 7 one day, and after passing through very stringent security I was met with the sight of the full size Millennium Falcon. My knees buckled. I was very lucky to have had these experiences, but Disney... Ugh.

I think the main problem with TFA, is that it doesn't introduce the characters, or connect the characters to one another, leaving them as cardboard... The initial introductions, those first moments are impactful with Finn and Rey, but TFA's big, big problem is... it never goes in on any of the chekov's guns it loads.

When I saw it a second time, I realised it had no compelling plot. Just fast transitions where JJ was afraid to let up on the action. And he never embedded his idea's into the narrative in a way that made those ideas integral to the continuation of the story...

As such, RJ could toss away lightsabers, because JJ never had Luke accept the Lightsaber. JJ created possibilities... but he allowed RJ to decide what those chances for story meant, and he tossed the Chekov's guns into the ocean.

replies.author: @RyanTheDark-o7z
replies.text: ​​​​@davfree9732Exactly. I rewatched TFA a few years ago and pointed out a crucial flaw with the trio of characters. Poe should have never been knocked out in the desert and met up with Rey along with Finn. Instead of having Rey be good at everything, Poe being the smart-mouthed pilot, Rey being the snippy straggler, and Finn being the darker, more ground to character, conceptually could have worked. But instead, they knocked Poe out, basically just so Rey could have a reason to look cool and take the best pilot's aspects, and bring him back later for no reason whatsoever. I wasn't even a big fan of the mystery boxes as I was in 2015 when I was 10/11 going in for these movies and wondering who Rey (Rey who was originally name Kira Solo and obviously meant to be the daughter of Han and Leia) and Snoke (who was supposed to be Darth Plagueis as much as they botched trying to cover this up because JJ hates the Prequels but were a big fan of the Plagueis scene) was. BB8 was meant to be the replaced R2-D2 even though he could do anything BB8 did in the film and pretty much nothing changes. There's no establishing the characters, no getting a feel for them, no building them up. Just action after action and a rehash of a story that was already told. TFA tries to make you ask these questions that didn't need to be answered and were only entertaining at the time but quickly fell apart conceptually at the seams when it came to rewatching a few times. If I was in charge of these movies, I would just have no mystery box, and use that time creating questions that didn't need to be asked into developing the characters and expanding the Star Wars base and creating a new journey instead of an old one that have already been told in other formats, and is by now, uncreative.

author: @MurderMostFowl
text: I have a good friend that worked for Bad Robot for many years. From the way he talked about things I think that Abrams had some strong insight into movie formulas that worked for bringing in the audience and crowd pleasing. He wasn’t a stellar author, and he wasn’t really an artist… He just knew what art looked like at a surface level. And what it needed to make it serviceable. He made his movies what he got out of other people‘s movies: entertainment. What true artists pick up as a reluctant side skill… how to make the business deal happen, is what JJ made his core philosophy. Commoditization of movies. Robbing story of passion and thought for eye candy. What I think they didn’t anticipate is what would happen when we got tired of the formula.

author: @BillPeschel
text:
JJ's fall was already in place as early as "Lost." He gave an interview after the first season in which he admitted that ABC told him to run the series for as long as possible. Instead of telling them to piss up a rope and create a tight show that ended on a high note (like, say, "The Prisoner"), he dragged it out as long as he could, then shat out a final episode that was so bad it killed any interest in the series (and I remember the first season and it was freakin' awesome).

Then came the Mystery Box speech, in which he talked about keeping the audience engaged with intriguing questions that would never be answered, and you can see that as soon as audiences realized he was all foreplay and no climax, he would crash and burn. The man has absolutely no storytelling chops.

replies.text: In the first season it was obvious that some of the show's mysteries came from the writings of Robert Anton Wilson and I thought all the mysteries in the show would go somewhere but they never did. After reading your comment I realized that's *VERY* appropriate for a work influenced by Wilson because his whole thing was to highlight absurdities, "damned data", and supposed mysteries to get people to think more critically about the world around them. It was never clear whether or not he believed in any of the things he brought up because his point wasn't to tell anyone what they _should_ think. In a similar vein _Lost_ was a show based around never telling anyone what the hell the answers were supposed to be.

replies.text:
That would be a "crash and burn AND resurrection", which perhaps wouldn't be a bad sort of episode for the series. To be fair, fans and the media got the VERY wrong idea about what MNS was based on the perfection of The Sixth Sense, so his "crash" was more about people looking for so much more from him than ever should have been the case. I've landed on the conclusion that he fits in whatever category one would place a Wes Anderson, someone who makes a very particular (and unmistakably the product of its creator) sort of film that will always work for its specific fans and not be understood (for all the right/fair reasons) by "general audiences".

Hollywood is always looking for the next Spielberg, a guy who almost can't help but satisfy general audiences, and in their hurry to crown the next one they too often force the label.

author: @whatbrick
text:
I was 2 years old when I first saw Star Wars in a theater in 1977, so I don't really recall that much from it. I went on to see it and the original trilogy a lot more over the years, over a hundred times at least for each film. Watched the prequel trilogy multiple times, though not as much as the original. Played most of the games, read a lot of the books, collected a lot of the toys and even watched the Holiday Special and cheesy ewok-based TV productions.

Needless to say, Star Wars had made me a fan.

I walked out of the theater after watching The Force Awakens once and thought "Well, I don't need to care about Star Wars ever again." I have since never watched another Star Wars movie or television show, haven't played any of the more recent games and haven't read any of the more recent books. My only foray into Star Wars land is occasionally playing a bit of The Old Republic MMO.

So "Thanks, JJ Abrams!" for curing me of my Star Wars fandom. Bet Disney loves the f**k out of that accomplishment.

replies.author: @THX--nn5bu
replies.text: I was 15 yrs old in 1977 when Star Wars was released, as a teenager, I was deeply obsessed with Star Wars, I attended the Celebrations 3 and 4 conventions back in the early 2000s, David Prowse and Jeremy Bulloch autographed my New Hope movie poster for me, I drank coffee at a Starbucks across the street from the Indianapolis convention center with Mr. Bulloch, what a fine gentleman, RIP my dear Sir, also I shared an adult beverage with the person that played Admiral Ozzel, on the last day of Celebration 3, another fine British gentleman. I have about 6 or 7 big plastic containers of action figures and other space vessels, two FX lightsabers, a shit ton of books and manuals, a plethora of VHS video, DVD/Blu-ray movies and audio soundtrack media of Star Wars episodes of the Original trilogy and the prequels, and also the OT music soundtrack on vinyl records. When 2015 arrived, I felt like I went through a divorce, it all came crashing down, gone are the good old days....sorry for the long reply.

T.), a group of friends band together for an adventure without the knowledge of their parents (The Goonies), the monster attacks (Jaws) but the authorities don't want to believe it's happening (Jaws). In the end evacuations happen (Close Encounters) and the army is sent end for battle (War of the Worlds), the army though prove useless. In the end the monster turns out to not be a monster, several abducted people are freed (Close Encounters) before the alien builds a ship and returns home (E. T).

author: @mandalorianmanic9525
text:
I *still* recall my disappointment in The Force Awakens. I was a child in 2005 and stranded on the far side of the world (long story) and the release of Episode 3 slipped me by, despite growing up watching 4-6 and adoring 1 and 2. So to get to watch a STAR WARS film IN THEATRES like my dad? Well, I was stoked.

Went with my brother and dad. Watched it. And walked out of the theater to a resounding "Eh."
And when everyone I knew was ranting and raving about it, I found myself totally isolated in not liking it, and in fact starting to dislike Disney Star Wars. I know it's a typical "I hated it before it was cool story" but in my circle, I *was* the first one to be a hater, and I got to watch as everyone one-by-one started hating it too.

replies.author: @nymasajista9207
replies.text:
​@AntiguaandTuvaluhe was far from unknown as Lost was one of the hottest shows on the planet at the time, as well as many people still having fond memories of Alias. Hence his name being the most prominent thing about all marketing of the movie.

True that shaky cam wasn't as big then but while watching the movie I kept thinking 'why are they doing the Blair Witch shaky cam bull**** that I didn't like in that movie.'

Yeah the few monster scenes were cool but always cut away quickly back to the stupid yuppie drama with the characters I didn't like or have a positive emotional connection with.

I didn't like that nothing about the monsters was explained or expanded on. Nowadays I understand that it's the 'mystery box' thing where the audience will make up what they want in their head to cover up for the parts of the story that aren't written and told to us.

While I don't like the movie I can understand why other would as many people would love to be young, rich, and pretty in NYC with main character status. I disliked that it was sold as a kaiju movie when it really isn't.
